words.shortDefinition: A browser technique for exchanging data with a server in the background without reloading the page.
words.simpleDefinition: AJAX — Asynchronous JavaScript and XML — lets a webpage send and receive data from a server without a full page reload. This makes interfaces feel faster and more interactive because only the changed portion of the page updates, not everything.
words.longDefinition: AJAX was coined by Jesse James Garrett in 2005 to describe an existing browser capability: using XMLHttpRequest to make background HTTP requests from JavaScript. Despite the acronym, JSON has almost entirely replaced XML as the data format. The technique underpinned the first wave of dynamic web apps — Gmail, Google Maps, and early social feeds all relied on it. Today, the Fetch API is the modern equivalent, but the underlying pattern is identical: issue a network request, receive data, update the DOM without navigating. AJAX blurred the line between static websites and desktop applications.
words.techContext: Modern AJAX is written with fetch() or axios rather than the old XMLHttpRequest API. Single-page application frameworks like React and Vue make AJAX calls constantly to hydrate components. Debouncing, loading states, and error boundaries are essential companions when building AJAX-heavy UIs.
words.exampleSentence: We used AJAX to load autocomplete suggestions as the user typed, so results appeared without refreshing the search page.
words.etymology: Acronym for Asynchronous JavaScript and XML; named by Jesse James Garrett in a 2005 essay, though the underlying XMLHttpRequest technique dates to Internet Explorer 5 (1999).
words.connector: Think of AJAX like a waiter who quietly refills your water mid-meal — the update happens in the background without interrupting the whole dining experience.

words.shortDefinition: A web application that loads a single HTML shell and dynamically renders all views with JavaScript.
words.simpleDefinition: SPA stands for Single-Page Application. The browser loads one HTML document once, and all subsequent navigation is handled by JavaScript — swapping out content and updating the URL without ever requesting a new HTML page from the server. This creates a fluid, app-like feel.
words.longDefinition: In an SPA, the server delivers a minimal HTML file containing a JavaScript bundle. The framework — React, Vue, Angular, or Svelte — takes over from there, intercepting link clicks and rendering components client-side. API calls fetch data; a client-side router updates the URL bar via the History API. The advantages are instant navigation after the initial load and a rich interactive experience. The trade-offs are a larger JavaScript bundle, a slower first paint, and SEO challenges because crawlers may not execute JavaScript. Server-side rendering (SSR) or static-site generation (SSG) are often layered on top to address those gaps.
words.techContext: SPAs are the dominant model for web applications built with React, Vue, or Angular. Code splitting, lazy loading routes, and service workers for offline support are standard techniques for making SPAs production-worthy. Frameworks like Next.js blend SPA behavior with SSR to get the best of both.
words.exampleSentence: Our SPA felt snappy after the initial load, but we had to add SSR with Next.js before search engines could index product pages.
words.etymology: SPA stands for Single-Page Application; the term emerged around 2002–2005 as XMLHttpRequest-driven apps became common, and was popularized as React, Angular, and Vue matured in the 2010s.
words.connector: Think of an SPA like a digital magazine app — you download the issue once and then flip instantly between articles without re-downloading the whole magazine.

codeExample.code:
Browser requests /                      Server returns index.html + bundle.js

User clicks /dashboard                  JS router renders <Dashboard />
  -> no server round-trip               URL bar updates via pushState
  -> fetch('/api/stats') for data       Component re-renders with results

User clicks /settings                   JS router renders <Settings />
  -> still no full page reload

words.shortDefinition: A rendering strategy where the server generates complete HTML for each page request before sending it to the browser.
words.simpleDefinition: SSR stands for Server-Side Rendering. Instead of shipping a blank HTML shell and letting JavaScript build the page, the server does the work upfront — producing fully formed HTML that the browser can display immediately, even before JavaScript loads.
words.longDefinition: With SSR, a Node.js (or other) server executes the same component code that would normally run in the browser, producing a complete HTML string for each request. That HTML reaches the client with real content already in place — search engine crawlers can index it, and users on slow connections see meaningful content faster. After the HTML loads, a process called 'hydration' attaches JavaScript event handlers so the page becomes interactive. Next.js (React), Nuxt (Vue), and SvelteKit all support SSR alongside static generation. SSR increases server load and complexity compared to a pure SPA, so teams often choose it selectively per route.
words.techContext: Next.js getServerSideProps and the App Router with React Server Components are the most common SSR entry points for React teams. SSR is especially valuable for e-commerce product pages, news articles, and any content that must be crawlable. The performance metric most improved by SSR is Time to First Contentful Paint (FCP).
words.exampleSentence: We switched the product listing page to SSR so Googlebot could see the full item catalog without waiting for JavaScript to execute.
words.etymology: SSR stands for Server-Side Rendering; the term emerged to distinguish the traditional server-rendering model from client-side SPAs, and was formalized as a named technique when frameworks like Next.js popularized it around 2016.
words.connector: Think of SSR like a pre-cooked meal — the kitchen does all the work before the dish arrives at your table, so you can eat immediately.

words.shortDefinition: An HTTP response that instructs the client to request a different URL automatically.
words.simpleDefinition: A redirect is a server response that tells the browser to go to a different URL than the one it requested. The browser follows the instruction automatically and requests the new address. Redirects are used for URL changes, enforcing HTTPS, and post-login flows.
words.longDefinition: Redirects are expressed with 3xx HTTP status codes plus a Location header. The main variants are: 301 Moved Permanently (the resource has a new canonical URL; browsers and search engines cache this), 302 Found (temporary; browsers do not cache), 303 See Other (used after a POST to redirect to a GET — the Post/Redirect/Get pattern prevents form resubmission on refresh), and 307/308 (temporary/permanent, but preserve the original HTTP method). SEO implications matter: a 301 passes link equity to the new URL, while a 302 does not. Server-side frameworks like Express and Django provide helper methods; edge platforms like Cloudflare Workers can issue redirects before traffic reaches the origin.
words.techContext: The Post/Redirect/Get (PRG) pattern uses a 303 redirect after a successful form POST to prevent duplicate submissions when the user hits the browser back button or refreshes. Redirect chains — multiple redirects in sequence — hurt page load time and should be collapsed to a single hop.
words.exampleSentence: We added a 301 redirect from the old domain to the new one so inbound links and search rankings transferred without any broken URLs.
words.etymology: From Latin redirectus — re- (back, again) + directus (straight, guided); first formalized in HTTP/1.0 (RFC 1945, 1996).
words.connector: Think of a redirect like a mail-forwarding order — the post office automatically sends your letters to your new address without the sender needing to know it changed.

codeExample.code:
HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: https://www.example.com/new-path
Content-Length: 0

# Browser automatically re-requests the Location URL

# 303 after a form POST (Post/Redirect/Get pattern)
HTTP/1.1 303 See Other
Location: /dashboard?success=1

words.shortDefinition: A login security method requiring the user to provide two or more independent proofs of identity.
words.simpleDefinition: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) requires users to prove who they are in at least two separate ways before granting access. The classic combination is something you know (a password) plus something you have (a one-time code from your phone). A stolen password alone is not enough.
words.longDefinition: MFA is based on the principle that a single credential is a single point of failure. The three recognized factor categories are: something you know (password, PIN), something you have (TOTP app like Google Authenticator, hardware key like YubiKey, SMS code), and something you are (biometrics — Face ID, fingerprint). Requiring factors from two different categories makes credential-stuffing and phishing attacks much harder to complete. TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password, RFC 6238) is the most widely deployed second factor — an app generates a six-digit code that expires every 30 seconds using a shared secret and the current Unix timestamp. Passkeys (WebAuthn) are increasingly replacing password-plus-TOTP flows entirely.
words.techContext: MFA is mandated by compliance frameworks such as SOC 2, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA for privileged accounts. Auth providers like Auth0, Okta, and AWS Cognito support MFA configuration out of the box. Developers should be aware that SMS-based MFA is the weakest form due to SIM-swapping attacks; TOTP or hardware keys are preferred.
words.exampleSentence: After enabling MFA on all admin accounts, the security team blocked a credential-stuffing attack that had compromised valid passwords.
words.etymology: From Latin multus (many) + factor (doer, agent) + Late Latin authenticatio (confirmation of identity); the concept formalized in the 1990s as internet banking grew.
words.connector: Think of MFA like a safe-deposit box at a bank — you need both your key and the bank's key to open it; one alone is useless.

codeExample.code:
TOTP flow (RFC 6238):

1. During setup:
   Server generates a shared secret (base32 string)
   User scans QR code into authenticator app

2. During login:
   User enters password          <- factor 1 (something you know)
   User enters 6-digit TOTP code <- factor 2 (something you have)

   Server computes:
     HOTP(secret, floor(unix_time / 30))
   and compares to user-supplied code

3. Code expires every 30 seconds — replay attacks fail

words.shortDefinition: An authorization model where permissions are assigned to roles, and users are granted roles rather than individual permissions.
words.simpleDefinition: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) attaches permissions to named roles — such as admin, editor, or viewer — rather than to individual users. Assigning a user to a role grants them all permissions that role carries. Adding or removing access is as simple as changing a user's role.
words.longDefinition: RBAC was formalized by NIST in the early 1990s and is the dominant authorization model in enterprise software. The model has four core elements: users (who perform actions), roles (named collections of permissions), permissions (allowances to perform operations on resources), and sessions (the active subset of a user's roles). Roles can be hierarchical — a Manager role might inherit all Viewer permissions. The key advantage over discretionary access control (DAC) is that permissions are managed centrally through role definitions rather than scattered across individual user records. RBAC is contrasted with Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC), which evaluates policies dynamically based on user, resource, and environment attributes.
words.techContext: In web APIs, RBAC is typically implemented by storing a user's roles in a JWT claim or database column and checking them in middleware before each route handler runs. Cloud providers — AWS IAM, Google Cloud IAM, Azure RBAC — all use RBAC as their primary authorization model. Principle of least privilege means users should hold only the lowest-privilege role that allows them to do their job.
words.exampleSentence: We introduced RBAC so editors could publish blog posts but could not delete user accounts — a permission reserved for the admin role.
words.etymology: From Old English rol (roll of paper listing duties) + Old English based (founded on) + Latin accessus (approach) + Latin controlare (to check against a register); formalized by Ferraiolo and Kuhn at NIST in 1992.
words.connector: Think of RBAC like a hotel key card system — housekeeping cards open service corridors while guest cards open only assigned rooms; the card's role determines access.

codeExample.code:
Role hierarchy:
  admin    -> can: create, read, update, delete users
             can: publish, delete any content
  editor   -> can: create, read, update own content
             can: publish own content
  viewer   -> can: read published content only

User assignments:
  alice  -> admin
  bob    -> editor
  carol  -> viewer

Check (middleware):
  DELETE /users/99
  requester role: editor
  required permission: delete:users  -> DENIED (403)

words.shortDefinition: An attack where an adversary forces a victim to use a known session ID, then hijacks their authenticated session.
words.simpleDefinition: Session fixation is a web security vulnerability where an attacker plants a known session token on a victim's browser before they log in. When the victim authenticates, the server reuses that same token — giving the attacker access to the now-authenticated session.
words.longDefinition: The attack proceeds in three stages: first, the attacker obtains a valid but unauthenticated session ID from the server (by visiting the login page, for example). Second, they trick the victim into using that exact session ID — via a crafted URL containing a session token in the query string, or by injecting a cookie through an XSS vulnerability. Third, when the victim successfully logs in, the server elevates the existing session to an authenticated state without issuing a new ID. The attacker, who already holds that ID, now has a fully authenticated session. The fix is straightforward: always regenerate the session ID immediately after a successful login. Frameworks like Rails, Django, and Spring Security do this automatically. Session fixation is catalogued in OWASP's session management guidelines.
words.techContext: The root cause is servers that do not regenerate session IDs on privilege escalation. Always call session.regenerate() (Node/Express), request.getSession(true) (Java Servlet), or the framework equivalent after login. Also ensure session IDs are not accepted in URL query strings — cookie-only delivery removes the easiest attack vector.
words.exampleSentence: The penetration test revealed a session fixation vulnerability because the login endpoint did not regenerate the session ID after authentication.
words.etymology: From Latin sessio (a sitting, a meeting) + Latin fixatio (a fastening); the term was formally described by Mitja Kolsek in a 2002 security paper.
words.connector: Think of session fixation like an attacker handing you a specific numbered locker key before you store your valuables — they already have a copy of that key.

codeExample.code:
Attack flow:
  1. Attacker visits /login  -> Server sets cookie: session=ATTACKER_KNOWN_ID
  2. Attacker sends victim a link:
       https://bank.com/login?sessionid=ATTACKER_KNOWN_ID
  3. Victim logs in with username + password
  4. Server authenticates user — but does NOT regenerate session ID
  5. session=ATTACKER_KNOWN_ID is now authenticated
  6. Attacker sends request with that cookie -> gets victim's account

Fix:
  After successful login, call session.regenerate()
  -> new session ID is issued; old ID is invalidated

words.shortDefinition: Application-defined HTTP headers that carry metadata beyond the standard headers specified in the HTTP specification.
words.simpleDefinition: Custom headers are non-standard HTTP headers that developers add to requests or responses to pass extra metadata — such as trace IDs, API keys, or feature flags — outside the URL and body. By convention they are prefixed with X- (though that convention is deprecated) or use a vendor namespace.
words.longDefinition: The HTTP specification defines a large set of standard headers (Content-Type, Authorization, Cache-Control, and others). Custom headers let applications attach additional metadata without embedding it in the URL or body. Common uses include: passing a request correlation ID for distributed tracing (X-Request-ID, X-Trace-ID), carrying an API key (X-API-Key), communicating rate-limit state (X-RateLimit-Remaining), or toggling server behavior (X-Feature-Flag). The X- prefix was originally mandated by RFC 822 but deprecated by RFC 6648 (2012) because it caused naming conflicts during standardization. CORS rules apply: custom request headers must be explicitly listed in Access-Control-Allow-Headers for cross-origin requests, or the browser's preflight check will block them.
words.techContext: Custom headers are a clean way to pass out-of-band information without polluting the URL or body schema. API gateways like AWS API Gateway can inject custom headers (X-Forwarded-For, X-Amzn-Trace-Id) automatically. Security-sensitive headers such as X-API-Key should always travel over HTTPS and should never be logged in plaintext.
words.exampleSentence: We added an X-Request-ID custom header on every response so support could correlate client error reports with specific server log entries.
words.etymology: custom: from Old French coustume (habit, established practice); header: from Old English heafod (top, leading part); custom HTTP headers have been possible since HTTP/1.0 (1996).
words.connector: Think of custom headers like sticky notes attached to a package — the main contents are inside, but the notes carry extra instructions for whoever handles it.

codeExample.code:
# Request with custom headers
GET /api/reports HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbG...
X-Request-ID: 7f3a9c2e-1b4d-4e8f-9012-abcdef012345
X-Client-Version: 4.2.1

# Response with custom headers
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
X-Request-ID: 7f3a9c2e-1b4d-4e8f-9012-abcdef012345
X-RateLimit-Limit: 1000
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 847
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1714867200

words.shortDefinition: The negotiation process at the start of a TLS connection where client and server agree on encryption parameters and exchange keys.
words.simpleDefinition: The TLS handshake is the opening ceremony of a secure HTTPS connection. Before any application data is sent, the client and server exchange messages to agree on a cipher suite, verify the server's identity via its certificate, and establish shared encryption keys — all in a fraction of a second.
words.longDefinition: TLS (Transport Layer Security) replaced SSL and is the encryption layer beneath HTTPS, WSS, and SMTPS. In TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018) the handshake takes one round trip (1-RTT) and works as follows: the client sends a ClientHello listing supported cipher suites and a key share; the server responds with its certificate, its own key share, and immediately starts sending encrypted data. The client verifies the certificate against a trusted Certificate Authority (CA), derives the same session key using the Diffie-Hellman exchange, and the connection is established. TLS 1.3 removed older, weaker algorithms and eliminated the two-round-trip overhead of TLS 1.2. Session resumption (0-RTT) allows subsequent connections to skip most of the handshake using a pre-shared ticket, though this introduces replay-attack considerations.
words.techContext: TLS handshake latency adds one or two network round trips to a cold connection — a meaningful cost for mobile users. HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 amortize this cost by multiplexing many requests over one long-lived TLS connection. Certificate transparency logs (CT logs) let anyone audit issued certificates, making mis-issuance detectable.
words.exampleSentence: The load balancer terminates TLS so backend services receive plain HTTP, meaning the handshake overhead happens only once at the edge.
words.etymology: TLS stands for Transport Layer Security — from Latin transportare (to carry across) + English layer + Latin securus (free from care); successor to SSL (Secure Sockets Layer), standardized as TLS 1.0 in RFC 2246 (1999) and updated through TLS 1.3 in RFC 8446 (2018).
words.connector: Think of the TLS handshake like two diplomats exchanging sealed envelopes of credentials before any sensitive conversation begins — trust is established before any secrets are shared.

codeExample.code:
TLS 1.3 handshake (simplified):

  Client  ->  Server:  ClientHello
                       supported cipher suites: TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, ...
                       key share: (curve25519 public key)

  Client  <-  Server:  ServerHello
                       chosen cipher: TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
                       key share: (server curve25519 public key)
                       Certificate (signed by trusted CA)
                       [Encrypted Handshake Data]

  Client verifies certificate chain against system trust store
  Both sides derive same session key via Diffie-Hellman

  Client  ->  Server:  [Finished — encrypted with session key]

  Application data now flows encrypted

words.shortDefinition: A protocol providing persistent, full-duplex communication between browser and server over a single TCP connection.
words.simpleDefinition: WebSocket is a protocol that keeps a connection open between a browser and server so both sides can send messages to each other at any time — without the client having to make a new request each time. It is ideal for live chat, real-time dashboards, and multiplayer games.
words.longDefinition: WebSocket (RFC 6455, 2011) begins as an HTTP upgrade request: the client sends an Upgrade: websocket header, and the server responds with 101 Switching Protocols. From that point the TCP connection remains open and both sides communicate using lightweight frames rather than full HTTP messages. This eliminates the overhead of repeated HTTP handshakes in polling scenarios. Messages can be text or binary. The browser's WebSocket API provides onopen, onmessage, onerror, and onclose event handlers. WebSocket connections are stateful, which creates scaling challenges — a load balancer must route a client to the same server instance (sticky sessions) or a pub/sub broker (Redis Pub/Sub, Ably) must fan messages out across instances.
words.techContext: WebSocket is the right tool for sub-second latency, bidirectional messaging — trading platforms, collaborative editors (like Figma), live sports scores. For strictly one-way server-to-client streams, Server-Sent Events are simpler and work better with HTTP/2 multiplexing. Socket.IO is a popular library that wraps WebSocket with fallbacks and automatic reconnection.
words.exampleSentence: We switched the order book from HTTP polling every second to a WebSocket feed and cut update latency from 1 000 ms to under 50 ms.
words.etymology: Web (World Wide Web) + Socket (from BSD Sockets API, 1983, itself from the telephony concept of a physical socket); standardized as RFC 6455 in 2011.
words.connector: Think of WebSocket like a phone call — once the call connects, both parties can speak whenever they want without either side hanging up and dialing again.

words.shortDefinition: A cookie attribute that restricts when the browser sends a cookie on cross-site requests, defending against CSRF attacks.
words.simpleDefinition: The SameSite attribute on a cookie tells the browser whether to include that cookie when the request originates from a different website. Setting it to Strict or Lax prevents a malicious third-party page from silently using a user's cookies to make authenticated requests on their behalf.
words.longDefinition: SameSite was introduced in 2016 and is now the primary browser defense against Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF). It has three values: Strict (cookie sent only on same-site navigations — the most restrictive; clicking a link from an external site will not send the cookie), Lax (cookie sent on top-level same-site navigations and safe HTTP methods like GET, but not on cross-origin POST — Chrome's default since 2020), and None (cookie sent on all requests, but requires Secure to prevent transmission over plain HTTP). The cookie specification that governs this behavior lives in RFC 6265bis. SameSite Lax is now the browser default when no SameSite value is specified, which broke some older authentication flows that relied on cross-site cookie delivery.
words.techContext: Session cookies for web apps should be set with SameSite=Lax (safe default) or SameSite=Strict (maximum protection). Third-party cookies — used for tracking and embedded widgets — must use SameSite=None; Secure, and browsers are progressively phasing them out. When setting cookies in an HTTP response header, always pair SameSite=None with Secure and HttpOnly for defense in depth.
words.exampleSentence: After upgrading to SameSite=Strict on our session cookie, our CSRF token middleware became redundant — the browser itself blocked cross-site POST requests.
words.etymology: SameSite: compound of same + site (from Latin situs, position or place); introduced as a cookie attribute in a 2016 Internet Draft, now governed by RFC 6265bis.
words.connector: Think of SameSite like a club membership card that only works when you show it at the club's own entrance — foreign venues cannot use your card on your behalf.

codeExample.code:
# Strict: cookie sent only on requests originating from same site
Set-Cookie: sessionId=abc123; Path=/; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict

# Lax (Chrome default): sent on top-level GET, blocked on cross-site POST
Set-Cookie: sessionId=abc123; Path=/; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax

# None: required for third-party / embedded contexts
Set-Cookie: trackerId=xyz; Path=/; Secure; SameSite=None

words.shortDefinition: The process of percent-encoding special characters in URL query parameters so they transmit safely over HTTP.
words.simpleDefinition: Query string encoding converts characters that have special meaning in a URL — spaces, ampersands, equals signs, and non-ASCII text — into a percent-encoded format like %20 for a space. This ensures the server can parse parameters unambiguously no matter what the values contain.
words.longDefinition: A URL query string begins after the ? character and contains key=value pairs separated by &. Many characters are reserved in URLs (: / ? # [ ] @ and others) or unsafe in certain contexts (spaces, quotes, angle brackets). Percent-encoding replaces each such character with a % followed by its two-digit hexadecimal ASCII (or UTF-8 byte) value — for example, a space becomes %20, a plus sign becomes %2B, and the euro sign becomes %E2%82%AC. JavaScript provides encodeURIComponent() for encoding individual values and encodeURI() for encoding a full URL. The browser's URLSearchParams API handles encoding automatically. The + character is an alternative to %20 for spaces in query strings (application/x-www-form-urlencoded encoding), but the two forms are not always interchangeable, which is a common source of bugs.
words.techContext: Forgetting to encode user input in query strings is a source of both bugs and security vulnerabilities. In JavaScript, always use encodeURIComponent() on values before building a URL manually. URLSearchParams is safer because it handles encoding automatically. On the server, frameworks decode query parameters before exposing them — reading req.query in Express already gives you decoded strings.
words.exampleSentence: The search broke when users typed a '&' in the query field because the frontend was not percent-encoding the value before appending it to the URL.
words.etymology: query: from Latin quaere (ask, inquire); percent-encoding specified in RFC 3986 (Uniform Resource Identifiers, 2005), building on earlier specifications in RFC 1738 (URLs, 1994).
words.connector: Think of query string encoding like packing a fragile item in bubble wrap — special characters are wrapped in a safe %XX format so they survive the journey through the URL unchanged.

words.shortDefinition: URI is the broad term for any resource identifier; URL is a URI that also specifies how to locate the resource.
words.simpleDefinition: A URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) is any string that uniquely identifies a resource. A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is a URI that also tells you how to reach it — including the protocol and network address. Every URL is a URI, but not every URI is a URL.
words.longDefinition: The distinction comes from RFC 3986. A URI has two subtypes: URLs, which identify a resource by its location and access method (e.g. https://example.com/page), and URNs (Uniform Resource Names), which identify a resource by a persistent name independent of location (e.g. urn:isbn:978-0-13-468599-1). In everyday web development the terms are used interchangeably and usually mean a URL. The structure of a URL is: scheme://authority/path?query#fragment. The scheme (https, ftp, mailto) determines the access protocol. The authority includes the host and optional port. The path identifies the resource within the host. The query string carries parameters. The fragment (hash) identifies a subsection within the resource and is never sent to the server. URIs can also be relative — /about, ./image.png — resolved against a base URI by the browser.
words.techContext: In browser JavaScript, the URL constructor (new URL(href, base)) parses and validates URL strings, giving structured access to .hostname, .pathname, .searchParams, and .hash. Using URL() prevents URL construction bugs that plague string concatenation. The location object in browsers exposes the current page's URL broken into the same parts.
words.exampleSentence: The spec clarified that the value in the href attribute is technically a URI, but since it includes the scheme and host it is also a valid URL.
words.etymology: URI: Uniform Resource Identifier — uniform (standardized), resource (from Old French ressource, aid, support), identifier (from Latin identificare, to make identical); URL: Uniform Resource Locator — locator from Latin locator (one who places); both coined by Tim Berners-Lee and formalized in RFC 1630 (1994) and later RFC 3986 (2005).
words.connector: Think of URI as the category 'identification document' and URL as a specific type — a passport — that also tells you which country issued it and how to verify it.

codeExample.code:
# A full URL (every part present):
https://api.example.com:8443/v2/users?sort=name&page=2#results
|----| |-------------| |--| |------| |--------------|  |-----|
scheme    host          port  path       query          fragment

# A URN (identifies by name, not location):
urn:isbn:978-0-13-468599-1

# Relative URI (resolved against base URL by the browser):
/about          -> https://example.com/about
./avatar.png    -> https://example.com/users/avatar.png

words.shortDefinition: The combination of a URL's scheme, host, and port that the browser uses to enforce the same-origin policy.
words.simpleDefinition: An origin is the triplet of scheme (like https), hostname (like example.com), and port (like 443) that the browser extracts from a URL. Two pages share an origin only if all three parts match exactly. The browser uses this to decide which resources one page can access from another.
words.longDefinition: The same-origin policy is one of the browser's most fundamental security boundaries. A document loaded from https://app.example.com:443 can freely read and write to resources from the same origin but is blocked from accessing data from https://api.example.com:443 (different host), http://app.example.com (different scheme), or https://app.example.com:8080 (different port). This prevents a malicious page from reading your bank's DOM or making authenticated API calls on your behalf using your cookies. Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is the mechanism by which servers explicitly opt into allowing specific foreign origins. The browser's fetch() and XMLHttpRequest both enforce the same-origin policy. The Origin request header is automatically sent by the browser on cross-origin requests, and the server's Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header declares which origins are permitted.
words.techContext: Understanding origin is essential for debugging CORS errors. When you see 'blocked by CORS policy', the browser is enforcing the same-origin policy and the server has not sent the correct Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. The null origin appears in sandboxed iframes and file:// documents and requires special handling.
words.exampleSentence: The API call failed because the frontend at https://app.example.com and the backend at https://api.example.com have different origins, triggering a CORS preflight.
words.etymology: From Latin origo, originis (beginning, source, lineage); adopted in the Web Origin Concept specification (RFC 6454, 2011) authored by Adam Barth at Google.
words.connector: Think of an origin like a home address — the browser checks scheme, host, and port the way a courier checks street, city, and ZIP code to confirm delivery.

codeExample.code:
# Same origin — all three parts match:
https://example.com/page-a
https://example.com/page-b          same origin (different path)

# Different origin — mismatch in one or more parts:
http://example.com/page             different scheme (http vs https)
https://api.example.com/page        different host
https://example.com:8080/page       different port
https://subdomain.example.com/page  different host

# Cross-origin request headers:
Origin: https://app.example.com

# Server response permitting that origin:
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com

words.shortDefinition: An HTTP header that specifies how long and by whom a response may be cached.
words.simpleDefinition: Cache-Control is an HTTP response (and request) header that tells browsers, CDNs, and proxies whether to store a response, for how long, and under what conditions it can be reused. Getting it right dramatically reduces server load and page load time.
words.longDefinition: Cache-Control was introduced in HTTP/1.1 (RFC 7234) as a replacement for the older Expires header. Common directive combinations: no-store (never cache — use for sensitive data like banking pages), no-cache (store but always revalidate with the server before using), max-age=N (cache for N seconds without revalidation), public (any cache — browser, CDN, proxy — may store the response), private (only the browser may cache; CDNs must not), s-maxage=N (override for shared caches like CDNs). A powerful pattern for static assets is max-age=31536000, immutable — cache the file for a year and never revalidate, because the filename includes a content hash (bundle.a3f9.js) that changes on every deploy. ETags and Last-Modified headers enable conditional requests, allowing a cache to revalidate cheaply with a 304 Not Modified response.
words.techContext: A misconfigured Cache-Control header is one of the easiest performance wins or security headaches to introduce. HTML pages typically use no-cache or short max-age values. Hashed static assets (JS, CSS, images) should use max-age=31536000, immutable. API responses that are user-specific must include private to prevent CDN caching of sensitive data.
words.exampleSentence: We set Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable on all hashed asset files so the CDN served them forever without hitting the origin.
words.etymology: cache: from French cacher (to hide); control: from Latin contrarotulare (to check against a register); Cache-Control introduced in HTTP/1.1 (RFC 2616, 1999), revised in RFC 7234 (2014).
words.connector: Think of Cache-Control like an expiry date label on food — it tells anyone handling the package exactly how long the contents stay fresh and who is allowed to serve them.

codeExample.code:
# Hashed static asset — cache forever
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/javascript
Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable

# HTML page — revalidate every request
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html
Cache-Control: no-cache

# Private user-specific API response — browser only, 5 minutes
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Cache-Control: private, max-age=300

# Sensitive data — never cache
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Cache-Control: no-store

words.shortDefinition: Writing HTML with elements that convey the meaning of content, not just its visual appearance.
words.simpleDefinition: Semantic HTML uses elements like header, nav, main, article, and footer that describe what the content is — not just how it looks. A screen reader, search engine, or another developer can understand the document's structure by reading the markup, without relying on CSS class names.
words.longDefinition: HTML5 introduced a rich set of semantic elements to replace the era of div-soup layouts. Key elements: header (site or section header), nav (primary navigation links), main (the primary unique content of the page — only one per page), article (self-contained content that could be syndicated independently), section (a thematic grouping with a heading), aside (tangentially related content, e.g. a sidebar), footer (site or section footer), figure and figcaption (media with a caption), time (machine-readable date/time), and address (contact information). Semantic markup benefits accessibility because screen readers use element roles to build a navigation tree. It benefits SEO because search engines weigh heading hierarchy and landmark regions. It benefits maintainability because the purpose of a section is clear from the tag alone.
words.techContext: A common mistake is nesting div elements to achieve layout without any semantic meaning. Use semantic elements first, then style them with CSS. Heading levels (h1–h6) must form a logical outline — do not skip levels or choose headings for their default size. The ARIA role attribute can add missing semantics to non-semantic elements, but using the right native HTML element is always preferred.
words.exampleSentence: Switching from nested div elements to semantic article and section elements improved our Lighthouse accessibility score without changing any CSS.
words.etymology: semantic: from Greek semantikos (significant), from sema (sign); HTML: HyperText Markup Language — coined by Tim Berners-Lee in 1991; semantic HTML elements formalized in the HTML5 specification (W3C, 2014).
words.connector: Think of semantic HTML like labelled rooms in a building — a door marked 'Kitchen' tells you what to expect inside, unlike a door marked only with a room number.

codeExample.code:
...
...
Home About

Post Title

November 1, 2025

Content here...

Related links
contact@example.com

words.shortDefinition: The modern browser interface for making HTTP requests using Promises, replacing the older XMLHttpRequest.
words.simpleDefinition: The Fetch API is a built-in browser function — fetch() — for making network requests. It returns a Promise, integrates cleanly with async/await, and provides a cleaner interface than the older XMLHttpRequest. It is the standard way to load data in modern web applications.
words.longDefinition: Introduced in the WHATWG Fetch Standard and now universally supported, fetch() accepts a URL and an optional options object for configuring method, headers, body, credentials, and cache behaviour. It returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object when headers arrive — importantly, it does not reject on non-2xx status codes, only on network failures. This is a common gotcha: you must check response.ok or response.status manually. The Response has methods like .json(), .text(), .blob(), and .arrayBuffer() that return Promises resolving to the parsed body. Aborting a fetch requires an AbortController. Credentials (cookies) are not sent cross-origin by default — set credentials: 'include' to enable this. On the server side, Node.js has included a native fetch implementation since version 18.
words.techContext: fetch() is the foundation of all data loading in modern React, Vue, and Angular applications. Libraries like axios wrap fetch with additional features (automatic JSON parsing, interceptors, request cancellation). The Request and Response objects in the Fetch API are also used in Service Worker intercept handlers and Cloudflare Workers, making it a universal abstraction across browser and edge runtimes.
words.exampleSentence: We refactored all XMLHttpRequest calls to use fetch() with async/await, cutting the data-loading code in each component by roughly half.
words.etymology: fetch: Old English feccan (to bring, go and get); Fetch API specified by the WHATWG Fetch Standard (whatwg.org/fetch), first shipped in Chrome 42 (2015) and Firefox 39 (2015).
words.connector: Think of fetch() like ordering from a restaurant — you place your order (the request) and get a ticket (a Promise), and the food arrives (the response) when it is ready.

words.shortDefinition: The browser API that lets JavaScript update the URL bar and manage navigation history without reloading the page.
words.simpleDefinition: The History API gives JavaScript control over the browser's address bar and navigation stack. SPAs use it to change the URL when the user navigates between views — so the back button works and links are shareable — without triggering a full page load from the server.
words.longDefinition: The History API is exposed via the window.history object. The key methods are pushState(state, title, url) — adds an entry to the history stack and updates the URL — and replaceState(state, title, url) — updates the current entry without adding to the stack. The popstate event fires when the user clicks the browser's back or forward buttons, giving the application a chance to render the corresponding view. The state parameter can hold any serializable data, allowing the application to restore UI state without a network request. Before the History API, SPAs used URL hashes (#/route) to avoid server round-trips, but this approach breaks server-side routing. All major client-side routers — React Router, Vue Router, Angular Router — are built on top of pushState.
words.techContext: Server configuration must serve the same HTML file for every URL path when using pushState-based routing, because a hard refresh or direct link will send the path to the server. A common mistake is forgetting to configure the dev server or Nginx fallback — leading to 404 errors on direct URL access. replaceState is useful for updating query parameters on filter changes without polluting the back-button history.
words.exampleSentence: The SPA router called pushState on every navigation so users could bookmark any page and the back button behaved exactly like a traditional website.
words.etymology: history: from Greek historia (inquiry, knowledge gained by inquiry); History API standardized in the HTML5 specification (W3C, 2014), introduced to address the limitations of hash-based SPA routing.
words.connector: Think of the History API like editing the chapter markers in an audiobook while you listen — you can update where you appear to be in the book without starting a new playback session.

words.shortDefinition: A suite of browser standards for creating reusable, encapsulated custom HTML elements without a framework.
words.simpleDefinition: Web Components are a set of browser APIs — Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, and HTML Templates — that let you define your own HTML tags with encapsulated behavior and styling. A custom button or date picker works in any framework because the browser itself understands it.
words.longDefinition: Web Components are built on three W3C specifications that work together. Custom Elements (customElements.define()) lets you register a new HTML tag backed by a JavaScript class extending HTMLElement, with lifecycle callbacks (connectedCallback, disconnectedCallback, attributeChangedCallback). Shadow DOM attaches an isolated DOM tree to an element — styles inside do not leak out, and outside styles do not bleed in, making components truly self-contained. HTML Templates (template and slot elements) provide inert markup fragments and named insertion points for composed content. A complete Web Component works in any HTML context and is compatible with React, Vue, Angular, and vanilla JS. Libraries like Lit (by Google) reduce boilerplate while staying on native APIs. Web Components are the foundation for design system tokens delivered across different framework teams.
words.techContext: Web Components excel in cross-framework design systems — a single button component can be used in a React dashboard, an Angular admin panel, and a plain HTML page without any conversion layer. The limitation is that SSR support for Shadow DOM (Declarative Shadow DOM) is still maturing. Browser support for all three APIs is now universal (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge).
words.exampleSentence: We shipped our design system as Web Components so the Android team using Angular and the web team using React could share the same button component.
words.etymology: web: from Old English webb (woven fabric, network); component: from Latin componere (to put together); Web Components standardized by W3C; Custom Elements v1 and Shadow DOM v1 shipped in Chrome 54 and Firefox 63.
words.connector: Think of Web Components like LEGO bricks — each brick is self-contained with defined connection points, and they snap together regardless of who designed the surrounding structure.

words.shortDefinition: An HTML element that embeds a separate web document inside the current page within its own browsing context.
words.simpleDefinition: An iframe (inline frame) lets you embed one webpage inside another. The embedded page runs in its own browsing context — its JavaScript and CSS are isolated from the parent. iframes are used for embedding maps, payment forms, video players, and third-party widgets.
words.longDefinition: The iframe element creates a nested browsing context with its own window object, history, and origin. Communication between the parent and the iframe is restricted by the same-origin policy: if the embedded page is on a different origin, the parent cannot access its DOM. Cross-origin messaging is possible via the postMessage API. The sandbox attribute is critical for security: adding it with no value or with selected permissions (allow-scripts, allow-forms, allow-same-origin, allow-popups) limits what the embedded content can do — preventing cookie access, form submission, and script execution by default. Other important attributes: src (URL of embedded document), srcdoc (inline HTML content), allow (Permissions Policy for features like camera and fullscreen), loading='lazy' (defer loading until near viewport), and title (required for accessibility). Overusing iframes causes performance and layout issues — each one is an independent page load.
words.techContext: Stripe and PayPal embed their payment form UI via iframe to keep card number input on their own origin — the parent site never touches PCI-sensitive data. YouTube and Google Maps embeds are iframes. The sandbox attribute should always be used for untrusted third-party content; add permissions back only as needed.
words.exampleSentence: We embedded the payment form in a sandboxed iframe so cardholder data stayed within Stripe's origin and we avoided PCI-DSS scope entirely.
words.etymology: iframe: inline + frame; frame: Old English framian (to benefit, to construct); the iframe element was introduced by Microsoft Internet Explorer 3.0 (1996) and later standardized in HTML 4.01.
words.connector: Think of an iframe like a picture frame hanging on a wall — the frame is part of your room, but the painting inside is a completely separate world with its own rules.

words.shortDefinition: Google's set of standardized metrics for measuring real-world user experience on the web, covering loading, interactivity, and visual stability.
words.simpleDefinition: Web Vitals are a set of performance metrics defined by Google that measure what users actually experience: how quickly the page shows meaningful content, how fast it responds to input, and whether things jump around while loading. They directly influence Google Search rankings.
words.longDefinition: Google introduced Core Web Vitals (documented at web.dev/vitals) as the metrics that matter most for user experience. The three Core Web Vitals are: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) — measures loading performance; the largest image or text block in the viewport must render within 2.5 seconds for a 'good' score. First Contentful Paint (FCP) — measures when the browser renders the first bit of content from the DOM; good threshold is under 1.8 seconds. Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) — measures visual stability; a score under 0.1 is 'good', meaning elements do not unexpectedly jump as the page loads. Additional metrics include Interaction to Next Paint (INP, the successor to First Input Delay) — measures responsiveness to user input. These metrics are collected in Chrome via the PerformanceObserver API and aggregated in Chrome User Experience Report (CrUX) data, which feeds Google Search's Page Experience signal.
words.techContext: Web Vitals can be measured in the lab (Lighthouse, PageSpeed Insights) and in the field (real user data via the web-vitals npm package or CrUX). Poor LCP is usually caused by large unoptimized images or render-blocking resources. Poor CLS is caused by images without explicit dimensions or ads injected above existing content. INP problems are usually caused by long JavaScript tasks blocking the main thread.
words.exampleSentence: After adding explicit width and height attributes to all hero images, our CLS score dropped from 0.35 to 0.04 and our Google Search ranking improved noticeably.
words.etymology: vital: from Latin vitalis (of or belonging to life, from vita — life); Web Vitals introduced by Google in 2020 (web.dev/vitals) as part of the Page Experience ranking signal.
words.connector: Think of Web Vitals like vital signs at a doctor's office — heart rate, blood pressure, and temperature give a quick, standardized picture of health without requiring a full examination.

words.shortDefinition: The browser's in-memory tree representation of a web page that JavaScript can query and modify.
words.simpleDefinition: The DOM — Document Object Model — is the live, in-memory representation of an HTML page that the browser creates when it parses HTML. JavaScript uses the DOM to read, add, change, or remove elements and attributes dynamically, which is how web pages respond to user actions.
words.longDefinition: When a browser parses an HTML document it constructs a tree of node objects — the DOM. The tree root is the document object; beneath it are element nodes (HTMLElement subtypes), text nodes, comment nodes, and attribute nodes. JavaScript manipulates the DOM via APIs like document.querySelector(), element.addEventListener(), element.setAttribute(), element.innerHTML, and element.appendChild(). Every change to the DOM can trigger style recalculation, layout, and paint — the browser's rendering pipeline steps. Frequent or large DOM mutations are expensive; batching changes (using DocumentFragment) or using requestAnimationFrame for visual updates reduces reflows. React, Vue, and other frameworks maintain a virtual DOM — a lightweight copy of the real DOM — and reconcile differences in batches, minimizing expensive real DOM operations.
words.techContext: Performance-sensitive DOM work involves avoiding forced synchronous layouts (reading layout properties like offsetHeight immediately after writing to the DOM), minimizing the number of reflows, and delegating event listeners to parent elements rather than attaching one per child (event delegation). Browser DevTools show DOM structure, applied styles, event listeners, and performance timeline.
words.exampleSentence: The animation was janky because each frame read offsetTop and then wrote a new style — causing the browser to recalculate layout on every iteration.
words.etymology: DOM stands for Document Object Model — document from Latin documentum (lesson, proof, official paper); object from Latin obiectum (something thrown before the mind); model from Latin modulus (small measure); the DOM specification was first standardized by W3C in 1998 (DOM Level 1).
words.connector: Think of the DOM like a live blueprint of a building — the architect can walk in and move walls or add rooms at any time, and the building updates immediately.

words.shortDefinition: A browser standard for receiving a one-way stream of server-pushed updates over a persistent HTTP connection.
words.simpleDefinition: Server-Sent Events (SSE) let a server push a continuous stream of updates to the browser over a single long-lived HTTP connection. The client opens the connection once and then listens; the server sends messages whenever it has new data. Unlike WebSocket, communication is one-way — server to client only.
words.longDefinition: SSE is defined in the WHATWG HTML specification and uses the text/event-stream MIME type. The server keeps the response open and writes data in a simple line-based format: lines starting with data: contain the payload; id: provides an event ID the client sends back as Last-Event-ID on reconnection; event: names a custom event type; retry: suggests a reconnection delay. The browser's EventSource API handles automatic reconnection — if the connection drops, the browser reconnects and sends the last received event ID, allowing the server to resume the stream. SSE works over standard HTTP/1.1 and benefits from HTTP/2 multiplexing. It is simpler to implement and proxy than WebSocket, and is an excellent fit for live dashboards, log tailing, AI chat streaming (where the server streams tokens one at a time), and notification feeds where only the server pushes data.
words.techContext: SSE is increasingly used for streaming AI-generated responses — OpenAI's Chat API and Anthropic's API both return streamed completions as SSE. SSE is simpler to scale than WebSocket because connections are stateless HTTP and load balancers handle them naturally. The EventSource API does not support custom request headers or POST bodies — for those requirements, the fetch() API with a ReadableStream reader is the alternative.
words.exampleSentence: We used Server-Sent Events to stream the AI-generated summary to the browser token-by-token, so users saw text appearing progressively rather than waiting for the full response.
words.etymology: server: from Old French serveur (one who serves); sent: Old English sendan (to send); events: from Latin eventus (outcome, occurrence); standardized in the HTML Living Standard (WHATWG), first implemented in Opera 9 (2006).
words.connector: Think of SSE like a radio broadcast — the station continuously transmits and your receiver listens, but you cannot talk back over the same channel.

words.shortDefinition: A browser API for storing key-value string data that persists indefinitely across sessions and tab closures.
words.simpleDefinition: LocalStorage is a browser API that lets JavaScript store key-value pairs in the browser permanently — the data survives page refreshes, tab closures, and browser restarts until explicitly cleared. Each origin gets its own isolated storage space of about 5 MB.
words.longDefinition: LocalStorage is part of the Web Storage API (WHATWG HTML specification). It is synchronous, meaning reads and writes block the main thread. The API surface is small: localStorage.setItem(key, value), localStorage.getItem(key) (returns null if missing), localStorage.removeItem(key), localStorage.clear(), and localStorage.length. Both keys and values must be strings — objects must be serialized with JSON.stringify and parsed with JSON.parse. Storage is scoped per origin (scheme + host + port), so https://example.com and http://example.com have separate stores. LocalStorage is not shared between tabs in real time — unlike SharedWorker or BroadcastChannel. It is appropriate for non-sensitive user preferences (theme, language, last-viewed item) but should never hold auth tokens or personal data because any JavaScript on the page can read it, making it vulnerable to XSS attacks.
words.techContext: A common misuse is storing JWTs or session tokens in LocalStorage — if the page has any XSS vulnerability, an attacker can read all stored values. Prefer HttpOnly cookies for auth tokens. LocalStorage is synchronous so large reads/writes in the main thread can cause jank; IndexedDB is the asynchronous alternative for larger or structured data.
words.exampleSentence: We used LocalStorage to persist the user's preferred color theme so it was applied before the first paint, avoiding a flash of the wrong theme on reload.
words.etymology: local: from Latin localis (of a place, from locus — place); storage: from Old French estorer (to store, lay up); Web Storage API introduced in the HTML5 specification, first shipped in Firefox 3.5 and Chrome 4 (2009).
words.connector: Think of LocalStorage like a notebook left on your desk — you can read it and update it whenever you like, and it is still there the next morning.

words.shortDefinition: A browser API for storing key-value string data scoped to a single tab and cleared when that tab is closed.
words.simpleDefinition: SessionStorage works like LocalStorage — same API, same string-only values — but its data exists only for the lifetime of the current browser tab. Closing the tab or the browser deletes all SessionStorage data. Opening the same URL in a new tab gives a completely separate, empty storage space.
words.longDefinition: SessionStorage is the shorter-lived sibling of LocalStorage in the Web Storage API. The key difference is scope: data is isolated to the specific tab (technically, the browsing context) that created it, and it is deleted when that browsing context is destroyed — i.e., when the tab is closed. Navigating away and returning within the same tab preserves the data. Duplicating a tab in some browsers copies the SessionStorage contents into the new tab's separate store. The API is identical to LocalStorage: sessionStorage.setItem(), .getItem(), .removeItem(), .clear(). Like LocalStorage, all values are strings — serialize objects with JSON.stringify. SessionStorage is useful for wizard-style multi-step forms (keep draft data scoped to the current session), per-tab state, or single-tab features where you explicitly do not want data to persist.
words.techContext: SessionStorage is a good fit for multi-step checkout or onboarding flows where partially entered form data should survive page refreshes within the session but must not carry over if the user opens a new tab or returns the next day. It is accessible to any JavaScript on the page just like LocalStorage — do not store sensitive data in it.
words.exampleSentence: We stored the multi-step checkout form data in SessionStorage so a page refresh mid-checkout did not lose the user's input, but the data cleared naturally when they finished or closed the tab.
words.etymology: session: from Latin sessio (a sitting, from sedere — to sit); storage: from Old French estorer (to store, lay up); SessionStorage part of the Web Storage API standardized in HTML5, shipping alongside LocalStorage in 2009.
words.connector: Think of SessionStorage like a whiteboard in a meeting room — everything written there is available for the duration of the meeting, but the board is wiped clean once the room is vacated.
